sections.title: Playing Together
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens with Bela introducing her family — brothers Banku Bhaiya and little Bishu — and their shared joy of playing chhupan-chhupai (hide and seek) and peek-a-boo outdoors. The family has planted a flowering garden where relatives, friends, and neighbours gather to sit, talk, and relax together in the evenings. The section establishes the home and its outdoor space as a site of togetherness and community.

sections.title: Singing Together
sections.content_summary: On rainy days the family gathers in a garden shelter to play antakshari and snakes and ladders, while the mother and Dadiji (grandmother) make hot pakodas and tea. Dadiji sings traditional songs about rain and has taught these to the children — an example of oral transmission of folk knowledge across generations. A rain-inspired folk song is included in the section.

tasks.task_text: Who are the members in Bela's family?
What do and your family members do when it rains?
Can you think of a rain song and sing it with your friends in the class?
Write the names of your family members in the table below

sections.title: Laughing Together
sections.content_summary: Dadaji (grandfather) plays traditional games with the children and participates alongside them, bridging generations through shared play. The family pet dog Shiru is presented as a full family member — loved, not hurt, and fed by the children. The section introduces the idea that families come in many sizes and types, all united by mutual love and care. Families also extend their love and care to animals such as cows, buffaloes, dogs, parrots, cats, and goats.

tasks.task_text: Do you know what games your grandparents used to play when they were young?
What games do you play with elders and other members of your family?
Which animals come to your home or your neighbourhood?
Have you ever shared food with the animals who come to your home? Share your experience.
Why do you think we should not hurt or tease animals?

sections.title: Helping Each Other
sections.content_summary: Every family member contributes to household work: Dadiji oils and braids Bela's hair while humming songs, parents share cooking and cleaning, Banku helps chop vegetables, and Bela helps Dadaji in the garden. Neighbour Kusum Mausi cooks festival sweets, and her daughter Munni makes rangoli using colours from dried flowers and leaves — an IKS craft tradition passed through the community. The section reinforces that children contribute meaningfully and that neighbours are extensions of the family.

tasks.task_text: Tick and colour the activities that you see in your family.
Collect some leaves and flowers that have fallen on the ground and create a rangoli.
How do you help your family?
Some people in the family need special care and support like grandparents, small children or someone who is sick. How do you help them?
Who is the oldest person in your family?
Who is the youngest person in your family?
Who is the tallest person in your family?
Do you have any members in your family who do not live with you? Who are they and where do they live? How often do you meet them?
What are the names of your friends who play with you at home or in your neighbourhood?
Look at the pictures below and write down Bela's relationship with all the members of her family.
Draw a picture of the members of your family and write your relationships with them. You can look at the example given on page 13.
Write down the names of relatives who visit your family. How are they related to you?
In Bela's family everyone does some work. Who does what work in your family? Find out and fill in the table on page 16.

sections.title: Fun with Family and Friends
sections.content_summary: Bela's family visits the park with Dadaji on a holiday and joins friends playing pitthoo — a traditional Indian team game involving stacked stones and a ball. Multiple generations participate together: Dadaji plays actively alongside the children. The outing ends with ice cream and laughter, illustrating how outdoor play and intergenerational participation strengthen community bonds.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: This closing section consolidates learning through four sub-tasks: two writing prompts about family love and games, a drawing activity of happy family moments, two discussion prompts about intergenerational learning and care beyond the family, and a word-search activity for game-related words. Together these invite children to connect the chapter's themes to their own lived experience.

tasks.task_text: How do family members show their love and care for each other?
What games do you play with your friends?
Draw some of the happy moments you enjoy together with your family.
What do you learn from elders in your family?
We care for many others who are not in our family. Who are they? How do we care for them?
Circle five words related to games in the grid below. One example is done for you.

dual_strand.natural: Human relationships, play, and mutual care within families and between generations
dual_strand.human_cultural: Cultural practices of togetherness — folk songs, traditional games (chhupan-chhupai, pitthoo, antakshari), rangoli-making with natural materials, and care for animals as family members
